part_title: Section Twenty-Four: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management
part_short_title: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management
part_subtitle: Final Operational IPM Protocol for Tuta absoluta

items.text: This protocol represents the operational synthesis of integrated management of Tuta absoluta in open-field tomatoes. It aims to convert the scientific principles presented in this chapter into an ordered sequence of practical steps beginning before planting, continuing throughout all crop-growth stages, and ending only after field removal and follow-up of remaining infestation sources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This protocol represents the operational synthesis of integrated management of Tuta absoluta in open-field tomatoes. It aims to convert the scientific principles presented in this chapter into an ordered sequence of practical steps beginning before planting, continuing throughout all crop-growth stages, and ending only after field removal and follow-up of remaining infestation sources.

items.text: This protocol should not be used as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This protocol should not be used as:

items: A single numerical threshold for all fields.
A substitute for the legal registration of products.
A substitute for the experience of the agricultural engineer.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A single numerical threshold for all fields.
[[prepared]]A substitute for the legal registration of products.
[[prepared]]A substitute for the experience of the agricultural engineer.

items.text: Rather, it should be used as:

items: A checklist for the technical team.
A basis for developing a field-specific programme.
A tool for evaluating seasonal success or failure.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A basis for developing a field-specific programme.
[[prepared]]A tool for evaluating seasonal success or failure.

items.text: The protocol is based on one fundamental fact:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The protocol is based on one fundamental fact:

items.text: The earlier intervention begins within the pest life cycle, the lower the need for costly corrective interventions close to harvest.
The protocol aims to achieve the following outcomes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The protocol aims to achieve the following outcomes:

items: 1. Prevent movement of the pest with transplants.
4. Target L1 and L2 before they enter deep tissues.
9. Manage resistance from the first application.
10. Comply with preharvest intervals and residue requirements.
11. Prevent old fields from reinvading newly planted crops.
13. Reduce control cost per accepted tonne.
15. Improve the programme from one season to the next.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Prevent movement of the pest with transplants.
[[prepared]]4. Target L1 and L2 before they enter deep tissues.
[[prepared]]9. Manage resistance from the first application.
[[prepared]]10. Comply with preharvest intervals and residue requirements.
[[prepared]]11. Prevent old fields from reinvading newly planted crops.
[[prepared]]13. Reduce control cost per accepted tonne.
[[prepared]]15. Improve the programme from one season to the next.

items.text: The programme should not be implemented if the following principles are absent:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The programme should not be implemented if the following principles are absent:

items: No traps without reading and maintenance records.
No spray decision based on traps alone.
No counting of old mines as current activity.
No intervention without identifying the target stage.
No use of an unregistered material.
No random repetition of an IRAC group.
No release of natural enemies without reviewing pesticide compatibility.
No harvest before completion of the preharvest interval.
No leaving infested fruits and residues inside the field.
No end to the season without eliminating the infestation source.
No evaluation of success from insect mortality alone.
No adoption of a foreign threshold without local calibration.
No use of a trade name in place of the active ingredient and mode of action.

[[prepared]]No spray decision based on traps alone.
[[prepared]]No counting of old [[term:mines]]mines[[/term]] as current activity.

[[prepared]]No release of [[term:biological-control]]natural enemies[[/term]] without reviewing pesticide compatibility.
[[prepared]]No harvest before completion of the preharvest interval.
[[prepared]]No leaving infested fruits and residues inside the field.
[[prepared]]No end to the season without eliminating the infestation source.

[[prepared]]No adoption of a foreign threshold without local calibration.
[[prepared]]No use of a trade name in place of the [[term:active-ingredient]]active ingredient[[/term]] and [[term:mode-of-action]]mode of action[[/term]].

items.text: The operational protocol for managing Tuta absoluta begins before tomato planting, not after mines appear.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The operational protocol for managing Tuta absoluta begins before tomato planting, not after mines appear.

items.text: The first steps towards success are:
In the nursery, movement of eggs and larvae into the field should be prevented. Spraying infested transplants and moving them without reassessment is insufficient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In the nursery, movement of eggs and larvae into the field should be prevented. Spraying infested transplants and moving them without reassessment is insufficient.

items.text: After transplanting, priority is given to detecting infestation carried with transplants or immigrating from field edges and managing it before a stable generation develops.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]After transplanting, priority is given to detecting infestation carried with transplants or immigrating from field edges and managing it before a stable generation develops.

items.text: Eggs and first- and second-instar larvae represent the most important intervention window, whereas spread of L3 and L4 indicates delayed monitoring, poor timing, or inadequate application.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs and first- and second-instar larvae represent the most important intervention window, whereas spread of L3 and L4 indicates delayed monitoring, poor timing, or inadequate application.

items.text: At flowering and fruit set, tolerance of infestation near growing points and clusters should decline. Once fruits appear, fruit inspection becomes a mandatory component of every round.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At flowering and fruit set, tolerance of infestation near growing points and clusters should decline. Once fruits appear, fruit inspection becomes a mandatory component of every round.

items.text: No treatment decision close to harvest should be made without reviewing:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No treatment decision close to harvest should be made without reviewing:

items: Yield that can still be protected.

items.text: The programme continues during harvest through:

items: Selection of an appropriate IRAC group.

items.text: Weak performance should not be managed by increasing the rate or repeating the group. It requires a technical investigation and susceptibility testing where necessary.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weak performance should not be managed by increasing the rate or repeating the group. It requires a technical investigation and susceptibility testing where necessary.

items.text: Protocol success results from integration of several small, continuous actions, not from one spray or an insecticide described as the strongest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Protocol success results from integration of several small, continuous actions, not from one spray or an insecticide described as the strongest.

items.text: Desneux, N., Han, P., Mansour, R., Arnó, J., Brévault, T., Campos, M. R., et al. (2022). Integrated pest management of Tuta absoluta: Practical implementations across different world regions. Journal of Pest Science, 95, 17–39. DOI: 10.1007/s10340-021-01442-8.

Biondi, A., Guedes, R. N. C., Wan, F. H., & Desneux, N. (2018). Ecology, worldwide spread, and management of the invasive South American tomato pinworm, Tuta absoluta. Annual Review of Entomology, 63, 239–258.

Guedes, R. N. C., Roditakis, E., Campos, M. R., et al. (2019). Insecticide resistance in the tomato pinworm Tuta absoluta: Patterns, spread, mechanisms, management and outlook. Journal of Pest Science, 92, 1329–1342.
Insecticide Resistance Action Committee. Tuta absoluta Insecticide Resistance Management Poster, Version 3.0.
Insecticide Resistance Action Committee. Tuta absoluta: Recommendations for Sustainable and Effective Resistance Management.
Insecticide Resistance Action Committee. Susceptibility Test Method No. 022: Tuta absoluta Larvae.
Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. Developing an Integrated Pest Management and Biorational Program for Control and Containment of the Invasive Pest of Tomato, Tuta absoluta.
